nativeRange.habitat: Slow-flowing blackwater rivers, rivulets, and seasonally flooded wetlands across tropical Central and South America. Typically found in soft, mineral-poor, acidic waters stained by tannins.

decorPreferences: high lighting (1 watt+ per liter, PAR 60+)
open background or midground placement with room for vertical growth

avoidWith: boisterous fish that knock stems loose

summary: Tonina is a delicate stem plant from the blackwater rivers of Central and South America, belonging to the family Eriocaulaceae. Its fine, whorled foliage makes it a valued accent in Dutch-style aquascapes and high-tech planted layouts, though it is generally considered one of the more demanding plants in the hobby.
careNotes: Tonina fluviatilis belongs to the family Eriocaulaceae and grows naturally in slow-flowing blackwater rivers and seasonally flooded wetlands across Central and South America, from Mexico south to Brazil, including the West Indies. Unlike the related Eriocaulon species, which grow as basal rosettes, Tonina has an upright stem habit with whorls of fine leaves arranged along a central stem. The plant is highly sensitive to parameter swings — sudden changes in pH, temperature, or hardness, as well as disturbance of the substrate, commonly trigger stem melt, particularly during the adjustment period when plants transition from emersed (above-water) growth to fully submerged conditions. Tissue-cultured stems are generally recommended over emersed nursery stock for more reliable adaptation. Tonina is best kept in a dedicated high-tech setup with soft, acidic water, high lighting, pressurized CO2 injection, and consistent fertilization; it tends to perform best alongside other soft-water, high-light, CO2-demanding species, away from herbivorous fish that may damage the delicate foliage.
breedingNotes: Tonina is propagated by taking cuttings of lateral shoots or stem tips, ideally no longer than around 5 cm, with developing roots already present. Trimming the upper portion of an established stem encourages the mother plant to branch laterally, producing multiple new shoots over time. Like other Eriocaulaceae, emersed-grown plants flower readily on tall stalks, but propagation from seed is uncommon in the hobby; vegetative cuttings are the standard approach.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary source for family (Eriocaulaceae), native range (Central/South America, West Indies, Mexico), pH (6-7.5), care level (medium but unsuitable for beginners), lighting (medium-high, 1 watt+ per liter), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (slow), placement (midground to background, Dutch-style), substrate (quality, mildly acidic), and propagation (cuttings ~2 inches with roots). Note: Buce's stated max height (1-4 inches) is unrealistic for adult submersed growth and was overridden using Aqua-Fish.net and hobby care guides; this likely reflects the as-shipped trimmed plant size.

additional.name: Flowgrow
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check used in place of Aquasabi (Aquasabi search returned no product page during research). Confirmed family (Eriocaulaceae), origin (slow-flowing blackwater rivers from Mexico to northern South America), temperature 18-29 °C, pH 4-7, kH 0-8 dKH, lighting high (1 watt+ per litre), CO2 20-40 mg/L mandatory, growth medium, placement midground, care 'very difficult', propagation by lateral-shoot cuttings ~5 cm. Fertilization targets: NO3 10-50 mg/L, PO4 0.1-3 mg/L, Fe 0.01-0.5 mg/L, K 5-30 mg/L.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Eriocaulaceae), South American origin, temperature 20-30 °C, pH 5-7.5, hardness 0-12 dGH, max height 35 cm (key reference for the 20-50 cm adult size range), lighting medium-high, CO2 essential, growth normal, placement background, propagation by side-shoot cuttings, care difficulty high (best for experienced aquarists), substrate fine gravel with nutrient enrichment, sensitive to chemistry changes, moderate flow preferred.
